Other activities include the Rotorua Bungy, a 43-metre jump over the Ngongotaha Stream; the Swoop, a giant sky swing reaching 130km/h; the Shweeb, the world's first human-powered monorail raceway; and freefall Xtreme, a vertical wind tunnel simulating skydiving. Multi-ride combo passes offer the best value and allow you to experience several activities in a half-day visit. Each ride includes full safety briefings and professional staff. Located five minutes from central Rotorua, it is easily combined with other Rotorua attractions. Suitable for ages 10 and up for most activities.

reviews.summary: A fantastic one-stop adventure venue with something for every thrill-seeker. The Agrojet is heart-pounding and the Shweeb is uniquely fun. Great value with combo passes.

pois.name: Skyline MTB Gravity Park
pois.short: Gondola-accessed mountain bike park on Mt Ngongotaha with downhill trails for all skill levels.
pois.commentary: Skyline Rotorua's MTB Gravity Park is one of New Zealand's premier mountain biking destinations, offering gondola-accessed downhill trails on the slopes of Mt Ngongotaha. The Skyline Gondola lifts riders and bikes to the top, where a network of trails descends through native bush and open terrain. Trails range from gentle Grade 2 flow trails suitable for beginners to demanding Grade 5 black diamond runs with technical rock gardens and big drops for expert riders. The park has hosted UCI Mountain Bike World Cup events, cementing its international reputation. Full bike hire and protective gear are available, and the on-site bike shop handles any mechanical issues. Uplift passes allow unlimited gondola rides for a session. The Redwoods mountain bike trails in the Whakarewarewa Forest are nearby, offering cross-country riding to complement the Skyline gravity trails. Rotorua is widely regarded as New Zealand's mountain biking capital.

reviews.summary: World-class mountain biking with the convenience of gondola uplift. Trail variety caters to all abilities. The combination of Skyline gravity trails and Redwoods cross-country makes Rotorua a mountain biking paradise.

pois.name: Whakarewarewa - The Living Maori Village
pois.short: Living Maori village where Tuhourangi Ngati Wahiao people have lived among geothermal features for centuries.
pois.commentary: Whakarewarewa is a living Maori village where the Tuhourangi Ngati Wahiao people have resided for over 200 years, using the geothermal activity for cooking, bathing, and heating. Guided tours led by village residents provide authentic insights into daily life, traditions, and the unique relationship between the community and their geothermal environment. You will see geysers, hot pools, and silica terraces while learning about Maori customs, history, and the practical uses of geothermal energy. Cultural performances include haka, poi, and traditional songs in the carved meeting house. The village shop sells locally made arts and crafts. Hangi meals cooked in geothermal steam are available. This is a genuine community experience, not a theme park, and residents' hospitality makes it deeply memorable. Tours depart regularly throughout the day. Located in the southern part of Rotorua city.

reviews.summary: An authentic and moving cultural experience guided by village residents who share their stories with warmth and humour. The geothermal setting adds a dramatic backdrop. A highlight of any Rotorua visit.

pois.name: Te Puia - NZ Maori Arts & Crafts Institute
pois.short: National centre for Maori arts featuring carving and weaving schools, the Pohutu Geyser, and cultural performances.
pois.commentary: Te Puia is the home of the New Zealand Maori Arts and Crafts Institute, the national school for traditional Maori carving (whakairo) and weaving (raranga). Located in the Te Whakarewarewa Geothermal Valley, the centre combines living Maori arts education with spectacular geothermal features including Pohutu Geyser, the Southern Hemisphere's largest active geyser, which erupts up to 30 metres high. Guided tours take visitors through the carving and weaving schools where students learn these taonga (treasured art forms), the geothermal valley with its boiling mud pools and silica terraces, and a recreated traditional village. Cultural performances featuring haka and poi are held in the carved meeting house. The centre also houses a kiwi conservation programme where visitors may see the nocturnal bird. Night-time tours offer a different perspective with geothermal features dramatically lit. Te Puia is a cornerstone Rotorua cultural experience.

reviews.summary: A comprehensive Maori cultural experience with the added spectacle of Pohutu Geyser. Watching carving and weaving students at work is inspiring. Cultural performances are powerful and authentic.

pois.name: Wai-O-Tapu Thermal Wonderland
pois.short: Rotorua's most colourful geothermal area with the iconic Champagne Pool, Devil's Bath, and Lady Knox Geyser.
pois.commentary: Wai-O-Tapu (Sacred Waters) is Rotorua's most visually spectacular geothermal attraction, featuring an otherworldly landscape of vibrant hot springs, steaming craters, and bubbling mud pools in colours spanning the spectrum. The Champagne Pool is the star attraction, a 65-metre-wide hot spring with vivid orange, green, and gold mineral deposits around its rim, constantly releasing carbon dioxide bubbles that give it its name. The Devil's Bath, a lurid green pool, and the Artist's Palette, with its soft pastel thermal features, are equally striking. The Lady Knox Geyser erupts daily at 10:15am when activated by surfactant. A self-guided walking track of about 3km loops through the thermal area, taking 75-90 minutes. Located 27km south of Rotorua on SH5, Wai-O-Tapu is the region's most-visited geothermal attraction. Arrive early to beat coach tour groups. Boardwalks provide safe access throughout.

reviews.summary: The most visually dramatic geothermal area in New Zealand. The Champagne Pool is even more impressive in person than in photos. Arrive early for the Lady Knox Geyser eruption and fewer crowds.

pois.name: Buried Village of Te Wairoa
pois.short: Archaeological site of the village buried by the 1886 Mt Tarawera eruption, with museum and waterfall walk.
pois.commentary: The Buried Village of Te Wairoa is an archaeological site preserving the remains of a village destroyed by the catastrophic eruption of Mt Tarawera on 10 June 1886. The eruption killed over 100 people and buried the village under metres of mud and ash, also destroying the famous Pink and White Terraces, then considered the eighth wonder of the natural world. The museum tells the story of the eruption through artefacts excavated from the ruins, photographs of the terraces, and accounts from survivors. A walking trail leads through the excavated village site, where foundations, walls, and personal belongings have been uncovered. The trail continues through native bush to Te Wairoa Falls, a beautiful waterfall cascading into a bush-lined gorge. The site offers a powerful connection to one of New Zealand's most significant natural disasters. Located about 15km southeast of Rotorua on the road to Lake Tarawera. The combination of history, archaeology, and natural beauty makes this a compelling Rotorua visit.

reviews.summary: A moving and educational heritage site that brings the 1886 eruption story to life. The excavated village is fascinating and the waterfall walk is beautiful. A compelling alternative to Rotorua's geothermal attractions.

pois.name: Mountain Jade Rotorua
pois.short: Watch artisans hand-carve pounamu and learn the cultural stories behind each design.
pois.commentary: Mountain Jade's Rotorua studio offers visitors the chance to watch skilled artisans hand-carve pounamu and learn about the deep cultural significance of different designs.

reviews.summary: Fascinating to watch the carving process. Staff explain the cultural significance well.

pois.name: Blue Lake / Tikitapu
pois.short: Crystal-clear lake surrounded by bush — excellent swimming, walking, and mountain biking.
pois.commentary: Blue Lake — Tikitapu — is a crystal-clear volcanic lake surrounded by native bush and exotic forest.

reviews.summary: Beautiful clear lake with a great walking track. Swimming is lovely in summer.

pois.name: Ōkere Falls & Kaituna River
pois.short: White water rafting on the Kaituna — includes the world's highest commercially rafted waterfall (7m Tutea Falls).
pois.commentary: The Kaituna River at Ōkere Falls offers one of the world's great white water rafting experiences. The highlight is the 7-metre drop over Tutea Falls.

reviews.summary: The Tutea Falls drop is absolutely unforgettable — pure adrenaline. One of the best rafting experiences in the world.

pois.name: Skyline Rotorua Gondola & Luge
pois.short: Gondola to summit with gravity-powered luge rides, zip-lining, and panoramic views over lake and city.
pois.commentary: Skyline Rotorua takes visitors by gondola to the top of Mount Ngongotahā, where panoramic views stretch across Lake Rotorua.

reviews.summary: The luge is addictive — kids and adults always want 'just one more run.' Views from the top are fantastic.

pois.name: Wingspan National Bird of Prey Centre
pois.short: NZ's only dedicated raptor conservation centre — flying displays with native falcon (kārearea) and morepork.
pois.commentary: Wingspan is New Zealand's only dedicated centre for birds of prey conservation.

reviews.summary: The flying displays are mesmerising — seeing a kārearea in action is thrilling. Important conservation work.

pois.name: Lake Ōkataina
pois.short: One of the most beautiful and least-visited lakes — surrounded by untouched native bush.
pois.commentary: Lake Ōkataina is often called the 'jewel' of the Rotorua lakes — a deep, clear volcanic lake almost entirely surrounded by native bush.

reviews.summary: Absolutely beautiful — feels like a hidden paradise. Kayaking on the still water is unforgettable.

pois.name: Whakarewarewa Forest Mountain Bike Trails
pois.short: One of the world's top mountain biking destinations — 150km of trails through towering redwoods.
pois.commentary: The Whakarewarewa Forest — home to the famous Redwoods — is also one of the world's premier mountain biking destinations, with over 150 km of purpose-built trails threading through towering Californian redwoods and native bush. The network caters to every level: gentle green-grade paths for families and beginners, flowy blue trails that make intermediate riders feel like heroes, and black-diamond tech that attracts World Cup racers. The volcanic soil drains fast, so riding conditions are good even after rain. Multiple entry points, bike hire and shuttle services make logistics easy. Rotorua hosted the UCI Mountain Bike World Championships here, cementing its status as a global riding capital.

reviews.summary: World-class trails that live up to the hype. The redwood forest setting is magical, and the trail variety means everyone from beginners to experts will find their perfect ride. Bike hire is readily available in town.

pois.name: Lake Tarawera
pois.short: Beautiful lake at the foot of Mt Tarawera — swimming, kayaking, and a natural hot water beach.
pois.commentary: Lake Tarawera lies at the foot of the volcano that tore its summit apart in 1886, and the combination of serene blue water and brooding, rift-scarred mountain makes for one of the most dramatic lake settings in the North Island. The lake is popular for swimming, kayaking, and fishing (rainbow trout to five kilograms). At the eastern end, Hot Water Beach is exactly what it sounds like — thermal water seeps through the sand, and you can dig a pool where cold lake water and hot spring water mix to your preferred temperature. Water taxis run from The Landing. The lake was sacred to Te Arawa, and the ruins of Te Wairoa — the buried village — are nearby.

reviews.summary: Stunning scenery and incredibly clear water. The hot water beach is a unique experience — digging your own hot pool where the lake meets thermal springs. The volcano backdrop is both beautiful and humbling.

pois.name: Wai-O-Tapu Lady Knox Geyser
pois.short: Daily geyser eruption at 10:15am — shoots water up to 20m. Part of the Wai-O-Tapu thermal wonderland.
pois.commentary: Lady Knox Geyser, located a short drive from the main Wai-O-Tapu thermal area, erupts daily at 10:15am after a park ranger adds biodegradable surfactant soap to trigger the eruption — a technique discovered accidentally by prisoners doing their laundry here in 1901. The geyser shoots a column of hot water and steam up to 20 metres into the air, and the eruption continues for about an hour as the chamber empties. A guided talk explains the geothermal science while you watch. It's theatrical, slightly absurd, and genuinely impressive — the kind of thing that could only happen in Rotorua. Arrive by 10am to get a good seat on the amphitheatre-style seating.

reviews.summary: The daily eruption is impressive, even knowing it's soap-triggered. The guided explanation is informative and entertaining. Arrive early — it's a popular attraction and seating fills fast.

pois.name: Tamaki Māori Village
pois.short: Award-winning Māori cultural experience — bus ride to a recreated village, performances, and earth-cooked hāngī feast.
pois.commentary: Tamaki Māori Village offers one of New Zealand's most immersive Māori cultural experiences. The evening begins with a bus ride into the forest, where you're formally welcomed onto the marae with a pōwhiri ceremony. Warriors perform the haka, and guides lead you through a recreated pre-European village explaining traditional customs, games, weaponry, and carving. The evening culminates in a hāngī feast — food slow-cooked underground in an earth oven using heated stones — which includes chicken, lamb, kūmara, and seasonal vegetables. The Tamaki brothers built this experience from scratch and have won multiple tourism awards. It's polished and theatrical, but the cultural content is genuine and the performers are outstanding.

reviews.summary: A genuinely moving and entertaining evening. The hāngī feast is delicious and the cultural performances are powerful. Worth every dollar — one of the highlights of any Rotorua visit.

pois.name: Agrodome Farm Experience
pois.short: Classic NZ farm experience — 19 breeds of sheep, shearing demo, farm tour, and more kiwi rural life than you can handle.
pois.commentary: The Agrodome has been running its famous farm show since 1971, and it remains one of Rotorua's most enduring attractions. The one-hour show features 19 different breeds of sheep paraded on stage (each trots out on cue and stands on its allocated platform — it's absurd and delightful), a live shearing demonstration, and a sheep dog trial. After the show, a farm tour takes you out on the property to hand-feed lambs, deer, and alpacas, and to sample local honey and kiwifruit wine. It's unashamedly touristy, but executed with such warmth and skill that even cynics leave smiling. A genuine piece of NZ tourism heritage.

reviews.summary: Cheesy in the best possible way — the sheep show is surprisingly entertaining and the shearing demo is genuinely impressive. Kids absolutely love feeding the animals. A classic NZ tourist experience.

pois.name: Hell's Gate / Tikitere Geothermal Park
pois.short: The most active geothermal park in Rotorua — Kakahi Falls (the Southern Hemisphere's largest hot waterfall) and mud baths.
pois.commentary: Hell's Gate — or Tikitere, as Te Arawa have known it for centuries — is the most volatile and active geothermal area you can visit in Rotorua. Where Wai-O-Tapu is colourful and photogenic, Hell's Gate is raw and aggressive: boiling mud pools throw grey sludge into the air, sulphurous steam roars from fumaroles, and Kakahi Falls — the largest hot waterfall in the Southern Hemisphere — pours steaming water over a wide rock face. The 20-hectare park has been a healing site for Te Arawa for over 800 years. After the walking tour, you can cover yourself in warm sulphurous mud at the Mud Bath Spa — a genuinely therapeutic (and hilariously messy) experience. George Bernard Shaw reportedly named it 'Hell's Gate' after visiting in 1934.

reviews.summary: Raw, dramatic, and genuinely impressive — this is geothermal activity at its most intense. The mud bath experience is unforgettable (bring old togs). Kakahi Falls is spectacular.

pois.name: Rotorua Canopy Tours — Zipline
pois.short: Zipline through ancient native forest canopy — an eco-tourism experience that funds conservation.
pois.commentary: Rotorua Canopy Tours takes you high into the canopy of an ancient native forest on a series of ziplines and swing bridges. The experience is thrilling — the longest zipline stretches over 200 metres through the treetops — but it's the conservation story that elevates it beyond a simple adventure activity. Revenue from the tours funds an intensive pest control programme that has allowed native bird populations to recover dramatically in the surrounding forest. Your guides are passionate about the ecology and will point out tūī, kererū, and kākā as you fly between platforms. It's one of those rare tourism experiences where your money genuinely makes things better.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary experience — the ziplines are exhilarating and the native forest is breathtaking. The guides are fantastic and the conservation story gives real meaning to the adventure. Worth every cent.

pois.name: Rotorua Duck Tours
pois.short: Amphibious vehicle tour through the city and straight into the lake — fun and unique.
pois.commentary: Rotorua Duck Tours operates a DUKW — a World War II-era amphibious vehicle — that drives through the streets of Rotorua before plunging straight into Lake Rotorua without stopping. The moment the duck hits the water is reliably hilarious, especially for children who can't quite believe a bus is driving into a lake. Once afloat, the tour cruises past Mokoia Island (the setting for the Hinemoa and Tūtānekai love story) and around the lake's geothermal features, including steaming vents visible from the water. The commentary covers history, geology, and Māori legend. It's cheesy, fun, and oddly informative — the sort of thing you do reluctantly and then recommend to everyone.

reviews.summary: Pure family fun — the splash entry into the lake gets screams every time. The commentary is entertaining and surprisingly educational. Don't overthink it, just do it.

pois.name: Lake Rotoiti Hot Pools / Manupirua
pois.short: Natural hot pools accessible only by boat on the shores of Lake Rotoiti — utterly secluded and beautiful.
pois.commentary: The Manupirua Hot Springs sit on the western shore of Lake Rotoiti, accessible only by water — there's no road in. Water taxis run from the Okere Falls end of the lake, and the short boat ride across is part of the experience. The springs emerge from the hillside into a series of natural pools of varying temperatures, surrounded by native bush that comes right down to the water's edge. On a misty morning, steam rises from the pools and the lake surface simultaneously, and the silence is broken only by birdsong and the occasional plop of a trout rising. It's the antithesis of a commercial hot pool experience — raw, natural, and unforgettable.

reviews.summary: Magical — soaking in natural hot pools surrounded by bush with only a boat ride to get there feels like a privilege. The water taxi ride adds to the adventure. Go early morning for the best atmosphere.

pois.name: Velocity Valley Adventure Park
pois.short: Multiple adrenaline activities in one venue — bungy, freefall, jet sprint, and the Shweeb (pedal-powered monorail).
pois.commentary: Velocity Valley packs more adrenaline per hectare than almost anywhere in the North Island. The Agrojet (a jet sprint boat through a narrow, twisting water channel at up to 100 km/h) is the signature, but there's also the Freefall Xtreme (a vertical wind tunnel), the Swoop (a 40-metre giant swing), the Shweeb (a pedal-powered monorail racer — the only one in the world), and a bungy tower. You can buy individual activities or combo passes. It's located at the Agrodome site on Paradise Valley Road, so you can combine extreme thrills with sheep shows — a very Rotorua combination. The Shweeb in particular is brilliantly weird and uniquely New Zealand.

reviews.summary: An adrenaline junkie's paradise with something for every thrill level. The Agrojet is heart-stopping and the Shweeb is unlike anything else on the planet. Combo passes offer great value.

pois.name: Kerosene Creek
pois.short: A free hot waterfall in the bush — soak under warm cascading water surrounded by native forest.
pois.commentary: Kerosene Creek is a geothermal stream 35 km south of Rotorua where warm water cascades over a small waterfall into a bathing pool surrounded by native bush. The water temperature is typically around 37–40°C. Free, uncommericalised. Note: avoid submerging your head due to amoeba risk.

reviews.summary: The quintessential free Rotorua experience. The bush setting is magical, especially on misty mornings.

pois.name: Rotorua Museum / Te Whare Taonga o Te Arawa
pois.short: A magnificent Tudor-style bathhouse in Government Gardens housing the stories of Te Arawa and Rotorua’s spa history.
pois.commentary: The 1908 Tudor-style Bath House in Rotorua’s Government Gardens is one of NZ’s most photographed buildings. Check current opening status due to earthquake strengthening work.

reviews.summary: The building alone is worth seeing. The Te Arawa galleries are excellent.

pois.name: Te Puia — NZ Māori Arts & Crafts Institute
pois.short: Home of the NZ Māori Arts and Crafts Institute, Pōhutu Geyser, and a living school of traditional carving and weaving.
pois.commentary: Te Puia sits in the Whakarewarewa geothermal valley and serves two roles: it’s a geothermal attraction (home to the 30-metre Pōhutu Geyser) and the campus of the NZ Māori Arts and Crafts Institute, where students learn traditional whakairo (carving) and raranga (weaving).

reviews.summary: The carving and weaving schools are genuinely impressive. Pōhutu Geyser is dramatic.

pois.name: Rotorua Museum / Government Gardens (Te Whare Taonga o Te Arawa)
pois.short: New Zealand's most photographed building — the 1908 Tudor-style Bath House in Government Gardens, currently undergoing restoration.
pois.commentary: Built in 1908 as the government's first major tourism investment, the Bath House was designed to lure wealthy visitors with curative geothermal waters. Its mock-Tudor facade sits incongruously and gloriously against the steaming lake edge, a symbol of colonial ambition meeting volcanic landscape. The building housed the Rotorua Museum until 2019 when earthquake strengthening work halted public access; it is expected to reopen around 2027 as a world-class cultural attraction. The surrounding Government Gardens — part croquet lawn, part rose garden, part thermal wonderland — remain freely accessible and are among the most evocative public spaces in New Zealand. Heritage New Zealand lists the Bath House as a Category I Historic Place.

reviews.summary: Even closed for restoration, the Bath House commands attention — a stunning piece of Edwardian architecture rising from manicured gardens on the edge of a geothermal lake. Visitors regularly describe it as one of the most photogenic buildings in New Zealand. The gardens and lakefront promenade make this a satisfying stop regardless of access to the building itself.

pois.name: Blue Baths Heritage Building
pois.short: A striking 1933 Spanish Mission–style bathhouse, the first in the world to allow mixed-gender bathing — now restored and operating as a thermal pool and event venue.
pois.commentary: The Blue Baths opened in 1933 amid considerable controversy: allowing men and women to bathe together in the same pool was scandalous by the standards of the day. The building's peach-and-white Mediterranean facade contrasts with the Tudor Revival Bath House just metres away, reflecting the eclectic ambitions of Rotorua's tourism planners. It closed in 1966 and fell derelict before a meticulous restoration reopened it in 1999. Today it operates as a geothermal pool complex and event venue, retaining original architectural details throughout. Heritage New Zealand holds it as a Category I Historic Place — the highest possible ranking.

reviews.summary: A beautifully restored piece of 1930s New Zealand tourism history, the Blue Baths offer a unique combination of heritage architecture and working geothermal pools. Most visitors are charmed by the building's story and the incongruity of its Hollywood-inspired glamour in the middle of Rotorua's Government Gardens. Worth a visit for the architecture alone.

pois.name: Ōhinemutu Māori Village
pois.short: A living Māori village on the shores of Lake Rotorua where geothermal steam rises between homes, a carved meeting house, and a lakeside church.
pois.commentary: Ōhinemutu has been continuously inhabited by Ngāti Whakaue since at least the 14th century, chosen for its strategic lakeside position and the abundant geothermal energy that residents still use for cooking and heating today. The name means 'place of young woman who was killed' in reference to a founding ancestor. Visitors can walk the foreshore, view the Tama-te-Kapua meeting house (from outside — it is not publicly accessible inside), and explore the extraordinary St Faith's Anglican Church. Steam rises between houses in an eerie, beautiful reminder that the village sits directly atop an active geothermal system. Respect for residents is paramount: this is a living community, not a display.

reviews.summary: Ohinemutu is unlike anywhere else on Earth — a real, inhabited village where geothermal steam drifts between homes and taonga Māori culture is lived daily. Visitors consistently describe it as one of the most authentic and moving experiences in Rotorua. Go with respect and curiosity.

pois.name: St Faith's Anglican Church, Ōhinemutu
pois.short: An intricately carved Anglican church where a famous stained-glass window shows Christ in a Māori cloak appearing to walk on Lake Rotorua.
pois.commentary: The current St Faith's was consecrated in 1914, replacing an earlier church on the same site from 1885. Inside, every surface tells a story: tukutuku woven panels, kowhaiwhai scrollwork, and deeply carved pou (posts) by Ngāti Whakaue master carvers line the walls. The east window — etched rather than painted glass — depicts Christ wearing a Māori kākahu (cloak), positioned so he appears to walk on the lake beyond, a breathtaking intersection of two spiritual traditions. Heritage New Zealand lists it as a Category I Historic Place. Sunday services remain a living cultural event, and visitors are warmly welcomed at other times.

reviews.summary: Consistently described as one of New Zealand's most beautiful and moving sacred spaces, St Faith's is a masterpiece of Māori-European artistic fusion. The Christ-on-the-lake window alone is worth the visit. Visitors should be mindful this is an active place of worship.

pois.name: Maunga Kākaramea (Rainbow Mountain) Crater Lake Walk
pois.short: A short DOC walk to a turquoise crater lake beneath vivid red-and-white geothermal cliffs — a hidden gem on the Thermal Explorer Highway.
pois.commentary: Maunga Kākaramea (Mountain of Coloured Earth) has been a geothermal reserve since 1903 and a landmark of cultural significance to local Māori. The Crater Lake Walk (1 km, 15–20 minutes) leads to an overlook above a turquoise crater lake ringed by striking red, yellow and white mineral-stained cliffs — a miniature, quieter version of the drama on offer at Wai-O-Tapu just kilometres south. The trailhead sits directly off SH5, making it effortless to visit on the Rotorua–Taupō drive. Longer tracks to the summit (3.5 km) reward with panoramic Waikato views. Entry is free; no permit needed.

reviews.summary: Consistently praised as one of the best free stops on the Thermal Explorer Highway — short enough for anyone, dramatic enough to satisfy serious walkers. The turquoise lake against coloured cliffs is genuinely photogenic. A must-do if passing on SH5.

pois.name: Kerosene Creek Hot Springs
pois.short: A free, natural geothermal stream with a warm waterfall pool, tucked off a gravel road on the Thermal Explorer Highway between Rotorua and Taupō.
pois.commentary: Kerosene Creek takes its name from the distinctive smell of hydrogen sulphide that drifts from its warm waters — though visitors quickly stop noticing. The stream runs at about 37–40°C year-round, creating a natural hot pool beneath a small waterfall that is one of the most photographed free swimming spots in New Zealand. Access is 2 km down Old Waiotapu Road (unsealed) off SH5, about 30 km south of Rotorua. Facilities are minimal — a gravel car park and a short bush walk — which keeps the experience refreshingly simple. Best visited on weekday mornings before the tourist coaches arrive.

reviews.summary: A genuinely special free-of-charge natural experience that rewards those willing to leave the main highway for a few minutes. The hot waterfall pool is warm, beautiful and free, which makes it justifiably popular. Arrive early or late in the day to enjoy it in relative peace.

pois.name: Thermal Explorer Highway Drive (Rotorua–Taupō, SH5)
pois.short: An 80-km drive through New Zealand's most geothermally active corridor, with multiple free and paid stops between Rotorua and Taupō.
pois.commentary: State Highway 5 between Rotorua and Taupō is branded the Thermal Explorer Highway for good reason: nowhere else in the world can you drive through such a sustained concentration of geothermal activity. Key stops south from Rotorua include Rainbow Mountain (free crater lake walk), Waikite Valley Hot Pools, Kerosene Creek (free hot spring), Wai-O-Tapu Thermal Wonderland, and the detour to Orakei Korako. The road also passes Atiamuri and the upper Waikato River gorge before dropping into Taupō's lakeshore. Allow a full day to do the stops justice, or three hours for a focused highlights run. The highway is also an official Ngā Haerenga Great Ride cycling route in sections.

reviews.summary: New Zealand's single most rewarding road for pure geological spectacle — an 80-km corridor of geysers, crater lakes, hot pools and coloured terraces. Travellers consistently rate it among the best drives in the country. The combination of free and paid stops means it suits all budgets.

pois.name: Waikite Valley Thermal Pools
pois.short: Natural geothermal hot pools fed by New Zealand's largest single boiling water spring — a relaxed family stop on the Thermal Explorer Highway.
pois.commentary: The Te Manaroa Spring discharges 60 litres of near-boiling geothermal water per second, making it the largest boiling water spring in New Zealand. Waikite Valley Thermal Pools channels this water into a series of outdoor pools at comfortable bathing temperatures, surrounded by native bush on a quiet valley floor 30 minutes from Rotorua. The facility is deliberately low-key compared to the big commercial thermal parks — there are no geysers or guided tours, just warm water, good steam, and a pleasant café. A DOC walking track follows the creek to the source spring. It is a deeply restorative stop on the Thermal Explorer Highway.

reviews.summary: A quieter alternative to the busy commercial thermal parks nearby, Waikite Valley offers genuine hot-pool relaxation in a natural setting. Visitors appreciate the lack of crowds and the quality of the water, fed by a world-record spring. Good café and clean facilities complete the picture.

pois.name: Orakei Korako (The Hidden Valley)
pois.short: New Zealand's largest geyser field — a dramatic geothermal valley reached by a short ferry crossing across the Waikato River, 25 minutes from Taupō.
pois.commentary: Orakei Korako translates as 'the place of adorning' — traditionally Māori women prepared and adorned themselves with geothermal silica for ceremonies here. The valley contains New Zealand's most impressive sinter terrace system (pink, white and yellow silica deposits), up to 35 active geysers, the Ruatapu Cave (a sacred sacred site of Ngāti Tahu–Ngāti Whaoa iwi), and boiling mud pools. Despite being partially drowned by the 1961 Ōhakuri dam, it remains the most spectacular geothermal valley in New Zealand that visitors can walk through. Access is via a free boat crossing across the Waikato River — the unusual entry adds to the adventure.

reviews.summary: Consistently outranks better-known thermal parks for authentic, uncrowded geothermal drama. The boat crossing, the scale of the silica terraces, and the sacred cave create an experience that feels genuinely extraordinary. Many repeat visitors to Rotorua and Taupō rate it as their favourite geothermal site in New Zealand.

pois.name: Rotorua Lakefront & Sulphur Point Walkway
pois.short: A free lakefront walk passing sulphur bays, public art, and sweeping views of Lake Rotorua and the caldera rim — the civic heart of the city.
pois.commentary: The Rotorua lakefront is the social and cultural centre of the city, a broad promenade running from the Government Gardens to Sulphur Bay where thousands of birds gather and steam vents hiss in the shallows. Public art installations are interspersed with volcanic rock gardens and view platforms. The Sulphur Point wetlands are a DOC-managed wildlife refuge supporting black swans, pied stilts, and the occasional kōtuku (white heron). In the evenings, the lakefront draws the city's diverse population — a living cross-section of Māori, Pākehā and the substantial international community that has made Rotorua its home. The famous rotten-egg smell is simply part of the experience.

reviews.summary: The Rotorua lakefront is free, accessible, and genuinely lovely — especially at dawn when the lake is calm and steam rises from the sulphur flats. Families, walkers and birdwatchers all find their version of the place here. The sulphur smell is famously polarising, but most visitors quickly adapt.

pois.name: Mitai Māori Village Cultural Experience
pois.short: An evening cultural experience combining a traditional pōwhiri, kapa haka performance, hāngī feast, glow-worm grotto, and a waka (war canoe) river display.
pois.commentary: Mitai offers one of Rotorua's most intimate and family-owned Māori cultural experiences, operating in a bush setting along the Waihou Stream north of the city centre. The evening begins with a ceremonial pōwhiri (welcome), followed by a guided glow-worm walk to a natural grotto, a waka taua (war canoe) river display performed by warriors, kapa haka performances, and concludes with a traditional hāngī meal cooked in geothermal earth ovens. The family-owned and operated model (four generations of the Mitai whānau) gives the experience an authenticity that larger commercial operations can lack. Guides provide genuine cultural context throughout.

reviews.summary: Mitai is frequently cited by visitors as the most authentic and intimate of Rotorua's evening cultural experiences. The family ownership is palpable in the warmth of the welcome and the quality of the storytelling. Combining glow-worms, waka, kapa haka and hāngī in one evening represents excellent value.

pois.name: Whakarewarewa Forest (Redwood Grove)
pois.short: A 5,600-hectare working forest with towering 100-year-old California redwoods, a 12-metre elevated canopy walk, and one of the world's best mountain bike networks.
pois.commentary: Planted from 1899 as a government experiment in exotic forestry, the California coastal redwoods (Sequoia sempervirens) of Whakarewarewa Forest have grown to extraordinary heights in the New Zealand climate — the tallest now exceed 70 metres. The Redwood Grove is the most visited section, accessible via a free walking track from the Visitor Centre or via the internationally acclaimed elevated Redwoods Treewalk (ticketed). Over 150 km of mountain bike trails wind through the broader forest, ranging from beginner-friendly circuits to world-class enduro trails. The forest also has cultural significance as a corridor connecting Rotorua's Māori communities to the lake and forested ranges.

reviews.summary: The redwoods are more magnificent than most visitors expect — the sheer scale of the trees creates an immediate, calming awe. The elevated tree walk adds a different perspective. Mountain bikers rate this forest as among the best trail networks in the Southern Hemisphere.

pois.name: Ngā Puia / Tikitere (Hell's Gate Geothermal Park)
pois.short: Rotorua's most aggressive geothermal park — bubbling black mud, the largest hot waterfall in the Southern Hemisphere, and therapeutic sulphur mud baths, with strong Māori cultural associations.
pois.commentary: Tikitere has been tapu (sacred and spiritually charged) for local Māori for centuries; the name 'Hell's Gate' was applied by George Bernard Shaw during his 1934 visit. The 10-hectare park contains Kakahi Falls — the largest hot waterfall in the Southern Hemisphere at 4 metres — alongside erupting mud pools, sulphur vents, and steaming lakes of intense activity. Ngāti Rangiteaorere, the tangata whenua (people of the land), run cultural tours through the park explaining the site's spiritual significance and traditional therapeutic use of the mud. The spa section offers sulphur mud baths and thermal pools claimed to have healing properties. The park is markedly less commercialised than Te Puia.

reviews.summary: Hell's Gate is the most viscerally dramatic geothermal experience in the Rotorua district — raw, hot, and genuinely intimidating in places. The mud baths are a highlight for those willing to immerse themselves in sulphur-rich volcanic clay. The cultural interpretation led by tangata whenua adds meaningful depth.

pois.name: Lake Rotorua Scenic Cruise / Mokoia Island
pois.short: A cruise across New Zealand's largest North Island lake to Mokoia Island, the setting of the famous Māori love story of Hinemoa and Tūtānekai.
pois.commentary: Lake Rotorua sits inside a volcanic caldera formed around 240,000 years ago and is one of the largest lakes in New Zealand at 80 km². Mokoia Island at its centre is the setting of the most celebrated Māori love story: Hinemoa swam the cold lake to be with her forbidden lover Tūtānekai, guided by his flute music. The island is now a kiwi sanctuary and wildlife reserve managed with local iwi, and can be reached by scheduled cruises from the Rotorua lakefront. The cruise itself provides sweeping caldera views and excellent commentary on Māori history, geothermal geology, and the contemporary life of the lake. Several operators offer morning and sunset options.

reviews.summary: The lake cruise to Mokoia Island combines natural beauty with one of the great stories of Māori oral tradition. The bird sanctuary aspect adds conservation interest, and the views back to the city and steaming lakefront are memorable. Best on a calm, clear morning when the caldera rim is visible.

pois.name: Hell's Gate / Tikitere
pois.short: Most active geothermal park in NZ with mud baths and the Southern Hemisphere's largest hot waterfall.
pois.commentary: Hell's Gate (Tikitere) is Rotorua's most active and dramatic geothermal reserve, named by George Bernard Shaw during a 1934 visit. The park features the Southern Hemisphere's largest hot waterfall, furiously boiling mud pools, steaming fumaroles, and sulphur-crusted vents across 20 hectares. The walking track takes about 45 minutes. After the walk, visitors can soak in the mud bath spa — natural mineral-rich mud applied to the skin before rinsing off in sulphur-fed hot pools. Tikitere has been used by the Ngati Rangiteaorere hapu for centuries for cooking, bathing, and healing.

reviews.summary: The most intense geothermal experience in Rotorua. The mud bath spa is wonderfully messy. Bring old clothes for the mud.

pois.name: OGO Zorbing / Rotorua
pois.short: Roll downhill inside a giant inflatable ball — a unique Rotorua invention.
pois.commentary: OGO is the original zorbing experience, invented in Rotorua in the 1990s and still the world's best. Riders climb inside a giant transparent inflatable ball and roll downhill on specially designed tracks, with the option of adding warm water for a sloshing ride or going dry for maximum tumbling. Multiple tracks offer different experiences. The concept sounds absurd but is genuinely exhilarating and appeals to all ages. OGO operates year-round and warm water rides make it enjoyable even on cold days. A quintessentially Kiwi invention.

reviews.summary: Ridiculous fun. Much more exciting than expected. The warm water ride is best. Multiple runs are inevitable.

pois.name: Ōhinemutu Māori Village
pois.short: Living Māori village on the lakefront where geothermal activity steams between the houses.
pois.commentary: Ōhinemutu is the original Māori settlement at Rotorua — a living village where Te Arawa people have lived for centuries alongside steaming geothermal vents, hot pools, and bubbling ground. The village is remarkable for the juxtaposition of active geothermal features with everyday life — steam rises from gardens, cooking pools simmer between houses, and the ground itself is warm underfoot. St Faith's Anglican Church is a must-see, featuring a unique window where the figure of Christ appears to walk on the waters of Lake Rotorua. The Tamatekapua meeting house is one of the most significant in the country. Visitors should be respectful — this is a residential community, not a theme park.

reviews.summary: A genuine living village with extraordinary geothermal activity. St Faith's church window is remarkable. Be respectful of residents.

pois.name: Whakarewarewa Forest — Redwoods & Mountain Biking
pois.short: World-class mountain biking and walking through towering California redwoods.
pois.commentary: Whakarewarewa Forest (The Redwoods) is Rotorua's most-visited natural attraction and one of the world's premier mountain biking destinations. The forest was planted with California redwoods in 1901 and now contains towering trees alongside native bush. The mountain biking trail network offers over 180km of trails ranging from gentle beginner loops to technically demanding downhill runs that host World Cup events. For non-bikers, the Redwoods Treewalk — suspended walkways between redwood platforms — provides a canopy-level experience. Forest walks through the grove of mature redwoods are free and spectacular. The trees are now over 120 years old and impressively large.

reviews.summary: World-class mountain biking in a stunning forest setting. The redwoods are magnificent. Something for all abilities.

pois.name: Ōkere Falls Track & Trout Pool
pois.short: Short bush walk to impressive falls with a glowworm cave and giant trout visible in clear pools.
pois.commentary: The Ōkere Falls Track is a short (45-minute return) walk through native bush along the Kaituna River to impressive waterfalls where the Okere River drops over rocky ledges. The track passes a small glowworm cave (bring a torch) and viewing platforms where enormous rainbow and brown trout are visible in the crystal-clear pools below the falls. White-water rafters can often be seen navigating the rapids and plunging over the falls — the Kaituna River has the highest commercially rafted waterfall in the world. The bush is lush and bird-filled. This is one of Rotorua's best free attractions and is rarely crowded.

reviews.summary: Lovely short walk with multiple highlights — falls, glowworms, giant trout. Watching the rafters is exciting. Free and uncrowded.

pois.name: Wai-O-Tapu Thermal Wonderland — Champagne Pool
pois.short: Spectacular geothermal park with the vivid orange Champagne Pool and Lady Knox Geyser.
pois.commentary: Wai-O-Tapu is arguably Rotorua's most visually spectacular geothermal attraction. The Champagne Pool is the undisputed star — a 65-metre-wide hot spring with vivid orange and gold mineral deposits around its rim, constantly effervescing with carbon dioxide (hence the champagne name). The park's 3km of walking tracks pass the Artist's Palette (a surreal landscape of coloured silica flats), the Devil's Bath (a vivid chartreuse pool), bubbling mud pools, and collapsed craters. Lady Knox Geyser erupts daily at 10:15am, induced by the addition of soap. The variety and intensity of geothermal activity here — combined with the extraordinary colours produced by different mineral deposits — make Wai-O-Tapu one of New Zealand's must-see natural attractions.

reviews.summary: The Champagne Pool's colours are otherworldly. Exceptional variety of geothermal features. Lady Knox is fun. Allow at least 2 hours.

pois.name: Tamaki Māori Village Cultural Evening
pois.short: Immersive evening of Māori culture with welcome ceremony, cultural activities, and hāngī feast.
pois.commentary: Tamaki Māori Village offers one of New Zealand's most comprehensive and immersive Māori cultural experiences. The evening begins with a bus journey to the village in the forest, where a dramatic welcome ceremony sets the tone. Visitors participate in cultural activities including traditional games, poi making, and carving, before a powerful cultural performance featuring waiata (songs), poi, and haka. The evening culminates with a traditional hāngī feast — meats and vegetables slow-cooked in an underground earth oven. The experience is designed to be interactive rather than passive — visitors are actively involved throughout. The forest setting and the warmth of the Tamaki family's hosting create a genuine sense of connection.

reviews.summary: Deeply immersive cultural experience. The hāngī feast is delicious. The warmth and authenticity of the hosting is exceptional.

pois.name: Hell's Gate Mud Bath & Sulphur Spa
pois.short: Bathe in natural geothermal mud and sulphur water — the only place in NZ where you can.
pois.commentary: Hell's Gate (Tikitere) is Rotorua's most active geothermal park and the only place in New Zealand where you can bathe in naturally heated mud. The geothermal walk passes the steaming Kakahi Falls (the largest hot waterfall in the Southern Hemisphere), violently boiling pools, and the Inferno — a lake of steaming, sulphurous water. After the walk, the mud bath experience is genuinely unique — sinking into warm, silky volcanic mud that's reputed to have therapeutic properties for skin conditions. A sulphur spa follows, soaking in mineral-rich hot water. The experience is both viscerally exciting and surprisingly relaxing. Historical records show Māori warriors used this mud for healing.

reviews.summary: The mud bath is an unforgettable experience. The geothermal walk is dramatic. Wonderfully therapeutic. You will smell of sulphur afterwards.

pois.name: Lake Tarawera Hot Water Beach
pois.short: Secret hot springs on a Lake Tarawera beach — dig your own hot pool in the sand.
pois.commentary: Lake Tarawera's hot water beach is one of Rotorua's best-kept secrets. On the eastern shore of the lake, geothermal water seeps up through the sandy beach, creating natural hot pools where the warm sand meets the cool lake water. Like its famous Coromandel namesake, you can dig your own hot pool and mix hot spring water with lake water to find your perfect temperature. The beach is accessed by boat (water taxi services operate from the landing), which adds to the adventure and ensures it never feels crowded. The setting — surrounded by bush-clad hills with views across to Mount Tarawera — is stunning. Bring a spade and a picnic.

reviews.summary: A secret hot beach that feels like a discovery. Boat access keeps crowds away. The lake setting is beautiful. Bring a spade.

pois.name: Skyline Rotorua — Gondola, Luge & Stratosfare
pois.short: Gondola ride to mountaintop with luge tracks, ziplining, and the Stratosfare revolving restaurant.
pois.commentary: Skyline Rotorua combines a gondola ride to the top of Mount Ngongotaha with multiple activities at the summit — luge tracks, a skyswing, ziplining, mountain biking trails, and the Stratosfare revolving restaurant. The luge — gravity-powered carts on three different tracks from gentle to thrilling — is the signature activity and endlessly repeatable. The views from the gondola and summit span Lake Rotorua, the city, and the surrounding forests and geothermal areas. The Stratosfare restaurant offers buffet dining in a slowly revolving room with panoramic views — particularly atmospheric at sunset. The gondola ride itself provides excellent views and a peaceful contrast to the activities at the top.

reviews.summary: Great variety of activities at the summit. The luge is addictive. Stratosfare dining views are spectacular. Something for everyone.

pois.name: Government Gardens & Museum Precinct
pois.short: Beautifully manicured gardens surrounding the iconic 1908 Tudor-style Bath House building.
pois.commentary: The Government Gardens are Rotorua's most photogenic precinct — immaculately manicured lawns, rose gardens, and bowling greens surrounding the iconic 1908 Tudor-style Bath House building (now the Rotorua Museum). The gardens were established in the early 1900s when Rotorua was promoted as a premier spa destination, and retain their Edwardian-era grandeur. Geothermal activity is visible within the gardens — steaming pools and sulphur-stained ground create surreal contrasts with the formal plantings. The Blue Baths building, croquet lawns, and the museum building create a remarkably preserved heritage precinct. The gardens are free to enter and provide a dignified counterpoint to Rotorua's more commercial attractions.

reviews.summary: Beautifully maintained heritage gardens. The Bath House building is iconic. Geothermal activity amid formal gardens is surreal. Free.

pois.name: Orakei Korako / The Hidden Valley
pois.short: Hidden geothermal valley with the most active geysers in NZ.
pois.commentary: Orakei Korako is often called 'The Hidden Valley' due to its off-the-beaten-track location between Rotorua and Taupō, and many consider it the finest geothermal site in New Zealand. Accessed by a scenic 5-minute boat ride across Lake Ohakuri, the reserve contains up to 23 active natural geysers, the largest collection in NZ, along with stunning silica terraces, bubbling mud pools, and the mysterious Ruatapu Cave with its jade-green pool. The 1.5km boardwalk loop takes about 1.5 hours and the elevated walkways provide safe, close-up views of the geothermal features. Far less crowded than Wai-O-Tapu or Te Puia, Orakei Korako offers a more intimate and arguably more spectacular geothermal experience.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently rate it above other geothermal parks for its diversity of features and lack of crowds. The boat ride adds excitement and the cave is a unique highlight.

pois.name: Ōkere Falls Scenic Reserve
pois.short: Thundering waterfall and rapids where Lake Rotoiti overflows to Ōkere Falls.
pois.commentary: Ōkere Falls Scenic Reserve protects a dramatic stretch of rapids and waterfalls where the Kaituna River drains from Lake Rotoiti. A short bush walk (30 minutes return) leads through native forest to viewing platforms overlooking the falls and the surging river below. The main falls drop through a narrow gorge, and the viewing platforms offer close-up perspectives of the thundering water. This section of the Kaituna River is also famous for commercial white-water rafting, featuring the 7-metre Tutea Falls — the highest commercially rafted waterfall in the world. Non-rafters can watch from the track as rafts launch into the rapids. Free access; the walk is suitable for moderate fitness levels.

reviews.summary: The falls are impressive and the short walk is beautiful. Watching the rafters plunge over Tutea Falls adds excitement. A rewarding free attraction.

pois.name: Sulphur Point / Sulfur Bay Boardwalk
pois.short: Free boardwalk through steaming geothermal vents on Rotorua's lakefront.
pois.commentary: The Sulphur Point boardwalk offers free access to active geothermal features right on the edge of Rotorua's city centre. The track leads from the lakefront through a landscape of steaming vents, bubbling mud pools, and sulphur-stained ground to Motutara Point on Sulphur Bay. The bay's warm, mineral-rich waters attract a variety of waterfowl and the area is a wildlife refuge. The boardwalk is well-maintained and provides safe passage over the thermal ground. It's a great introduction to Rotorua's geothermal activity without any entry fee, and it's easily combined with a lakefront walk. The sulphurous smell is strong but part of the experience. Best visited in cooler weather when steam is most dramatic.

reviews.summary: Visitors appreciate this free geothermal experience in the heart of the city. The steaming vents and mud pools are impressive, if smelly. Great for a quick lakefront walk.

pois.name: Eat Streat Rotorua
pois.short: Rotorua's vibrant dining precinct on the lakefront.
pois.commentary: Eat Streat is Rotorua's premier dining and entertainment precinct, a pedestrianised stretch of Tutanekai Street between the lakefront and the city centre. The street is lined with restaurants, cafés, and bars offering everything from craft beer and woodfired pizza to hangi-inspired cuisine and fine dining. The covered outdoor seating area is heated in winter and buzzing with atmosphere year-round. Notable restaurants include Atticus Finch for cocktails, Pig & Whistle in a heritage police station building, and Third Place Café for breakfast. Live music features on weekends. The precinct links directly to the lakefront, making it easy to combine dinner with a sunset walk. Parking is plentiful nearby.

reviews.summary: Visitors enjoy the variety of dining options and the lively atmosphere. The covered outdoor area works well in all weather. Good for both families and couples.

pois.name: Hamurana Springs
pois.short: Crystal-clear turquoise springs bubbling up beneath towering redwoods — Rotorua's most tranquil natural treasure.
pois.commentary: Hamurana Springs is a world away from Rotorua's geothermal drama. An easy loop walk threads through a grove of towering California redwoods and native bush to reach springs of impossible clarity, where cold water bubbles up from deep underground through white sand in shades of turquoise and emerald. The colour comes from the depth and purity of the water, which has been filtered through volcanic rock for decades. Early morning is the most peaceful time, when mist rises from the water and the forest is alive with birdsong. Entry is by donation.

reviews.summary: The water clarity is mesmerising. The redwood grove adds atmosphere. Best visited early morning for peace. Some note the walk is short but the beauty compensates.

pois.name: Tarawera Falls (Underground River)
pois.short: The Tarawera River vanishes underground and bursts through cliff faces in a dramatic display of geological theatre.
pois.commentary: Tarawera Falls is one of New Zealand's most unusual waterfalls. The river doesn't drop over a conventional cliff — instead, it disappears underground into volcanic rock and re-emerges from multiple points in a cliff face, bursting through fissures and caves in a spectacular curtain of water. The 1.5-hour return walk passes through beautiful native forest, and a permit from Ngāti Rangitihi is required (available online). The falls are particularly impressive after rain when the underground channels run full. This is a geological curiosity unlike anything else in the volcanic zone.

reviews.summary: A unique waterfall that amazes visitors. The bush walk is pleasant and well-maintained. Remember to get the access permit. Best after rainfall when the flow is strong.

pois.name: Lake Ōkataina Scenic Loop Walk
pois.short: A bush-fringed volcanic lake with excellent swimming, walking tracks and a fraction of the crowds that gather at Tarawera and Tikitapu.
pois.commentary: Lake Ōkataina is one of Rotorua's most beautiful lakes but receives far fewer visitors than its famous neighbours. The lake sits in a forested volcanic crater with bush growing right down to the water's edge. Several walking tracks explore the surrounding forest, ranging from 30-minute loops to the multi-hour Eastern Ōkataina Walkway. The swimming is superb — clear, calm water in a stunning setting. There's a small beach area at the road end with basic facilities. If the Blue and Green Lakes are busy, come here instead for the same volcanic landscape without the crowds.

reviews.summary: A peaceful alternative to busier Rotorua lakes. The swimming is excellent. The bush walks are well-maintained. Some note the access road is narrow and winding.

pois.name: Wingspan Raptor Flying Display
pois.short: Watch New Zealand's native falcon (kārearea) fly free in daily displays at this raptor conservation centre near Rotorua.
pois.commentary: Wingspan is dedicated to the conservation of New Zealand's birds of prey, particularly the kārearea (New Zealand falcon) — the country's only endemic raptor and one of the fastest birds on earth. Daily flying displays send trained falcons soaring and diving at breathtaking speed just metres from the audience. The centre also breeds falcons for release into the wild and runs educational programmes. The passion of the staff is infectious, and watching a kārearea stoop at speed is one of those primal wildlife encounters you don't forget. A small operation doing vital conservation work.

reviews.summary: The flying displays are thrilling and educational. Staff passion for conservation is evident. Some note the facility is modest but the experience is outstanding. Great for families.

pois.name: Sulphur Flats Free Geothermal Walk
pois.short: Free geothermal activity right in Rotorua — bubbling mud pools, steaming vents and sulphur flats along the lakefront.
pois.commentary: You don't need to pay for a thermal park to see geothermal activity in Rotorua. The Sulphur Flats area along the southern lakefront is a free, open geothermal zone where boardwalks wind past bubbling mud pools, steaming fumaroles and sulphur-crusted ground. The activity is genuine and impressive — this area sits on the same fault line as the commercial parks. The walk connects to the lakefront promenade and Sulphur Point, where hot water enters the lake and waterfowl congregate in the warm shallows. It's especially atmospheric at dawn when steam billows across the flats.

reviews.summary: A great free alternative to paid geothermal parks. The mud pools are impressive. The smell is strong but you get used to it. Stay on the boardwalks — the ground is genuinely hot.

pois.name: Tikitere (Hell's Gate) Mud Concert Pools
pois.short: The Kakahi Falls at Tikitere — the Southern Hemisphere's largest hot waterfall — plus therapeutic mud bathing in volcanic clay.
pois.commentary: Hell's Gate (Tikitere) is Rotorua's most active geothermal park, and its Kakahi Falls are the largest hot waterfall in the Southern Hemisphere, cascading 8 metres of steaming water over a series of terraces. The park is unique in offering genuine therapeutic mud bathing — you coat yourself in volcanic mud, let it dry, then rinse off in the sulphur spa. The experience is gloriously messy and oddly invigorating. The geothermal walk through the park reveals violently boiling pools, steaming craters and the distinctive 'concert' of bubbling mud that gives the area its character. Tikitere has been used therapeutically by local Māori for 700 years.

reviews.summary: The mud bathing is a unique and memorable experience. The geothermal activity is impressively violent. The hot waterfall is a highlight. Some find the smell strong. Allow at least 2 hours.

pois.name: Blue Lake / Tikitapu Walkway
pois.short: A bush-fringed walk around one of Rotorua's most beautiful volcanic lakes — stunning blue water, excellent swimming, and forest birdsong.
pois.commentary: The Blue Lake (Tikitapu) walkway is a 5.5 km loop around one of Rotorua's prettiest volcanic lakes. The track threads through mature native bush with frequent views over the intensely blue water — the colour comes from the lake's volcanic origin and exceptional clarity. Several access points allow swimming from sandy beaches and rocky ledges. The walk is undulating rather than flat, with some short climbs, but is well-maintained and accessible to most walkers. The birdsong in the bush is excellent — tūī, bellbird and fantail are common companions. On calm mornings the lake's mirror-like surface reflects the surrounding forest perfectly.

reviews.summary: A beautiful walk around a stunning lake. The blue water is remarkable. Swimming spots are excellent. Well-maintained track with some hills. Can be busy on weekends but rarely crowded.

pois.name: Okere Falls Scenic Walk
pois.short: Short walk past thundering waterfalls where Lake Rotoiti drains into the Kaituna River.
pois.commentary: This brief but spectacular walk follows the Kaituna River as it tumbles through a narrow gorge via a series of waterfalls. The path passes through lush native bush with views of the thundering cascade and the pool below — a powerful sight just off SH33.

reviews.summary: Impressive waterfalls on a quick, easy walk.

pois.name: Sulphur Point Wildlife Sanctuary
pois.short: Lakefront wetland sanctuary where birdlife thrives among steaming geothermal vents.
pois.commentary: Sulphur Point is a unique urban wildlife area where geothermal steam vents sit alongside wetland pools attracting diverse birdlife. Black swans, stilts, and scaup share the landscape with bubbling mud and sulphur deposits — an only-in-Rotorua juxtaposition.

reviews.summary: Fascinating combination of geothermal and birdlife.

pois.name: Kuirau Park Thermal Area
pois.short: Free urban park with boiling mud pools, hot lakes, and steaming vents.
pois.commentary: Kuirau Park is Rotorua's free geothermal wonderland, right in the centre of town. Boiling mud pools, steaming crater lakes, and hissing vents sit alongside playgrounds and picnic areas — a uniquely Rotorua experience where kids play beside volcanic activity.

reviews.summary: Free geothermal park right in town — great quick stop.

pois.name: Redwoods Nightlights Treewalk
pois.short: Elevated walkway through redwoods illuminated by artist-designed lanterns.
pois.commentary: The Redwoods Nightlights Treewalk suspends you among the towering redwood canopy on a series of bridges lit by 30 bespoke lantern sculptures designed by David Trubridge. The after-dark experience transforms the forest into an otherworldly spectacle of light and shadow.

reviews.summary: Magical night experience — the lanterns in the redwoods are stunning.

pois.name: Lake Ōkataina Scenic Reserve
pois.short: Pristine volcanic lake surrounded by dense native bush with walking tracks.
pois.commentary: Lake Ōkataina is one of the least developed of Rotorua's volcanic lakes, surrounded by dense native bush that comes right down to the water. Walking tracks link to neighbouring lakes, and the clear waters offer excellent swimming and kayaking in a wonderfully peaceful setting.

reviews.summary: Beautifully peaceful — feels like a secret lake.

pois.name: Kerosene Creek Hot Stream
pois.short: Free geothermal stream with a waterfall hot pool in the bush south of Rotorua.
pois.commentary: Kerosene Creek is a free natural hot stream flowing through native bush, with a small waterfall creating a perfect hot pool. Popular with locals, it offers an authentic geothermal bathing experience far from the commercial hot pools — bring your own towel and enjoy.

reviews.summary: Free hot waterfall in the bush — an amazing local secret.

pois.name: Lake Tikitapu Loop Track
pois.short: A 5.5km loop around Rotorua's Blue Lake, with beaches, bush, picnic areas and a lookout toward Rotokākahi.
pois.commentary: Lake Tikitapu is one of Rotorua's easiest lake escapes: close to town, ringed by bush-clad slopes and set up for swimming, picnics and the 5.5 kilometre Blue Lake Track. The loop passes beaches and forested edges, with a lookout between Tikitapu and neighbouring Rotokākahi. Rotorua Lakes Council notes that Rotokākahi is privately owned by local iwi and no swimming, fishing or boating is permitted there, which makes this walk a useful lesson in proximity and respect. Enjoy Tikitapu's open recreation, but read the surrounding lakes as different places with different responsibilities.

reviews.summary: Popular for an easy loop, swimming access and Rotorua lake scenery; visitors appreciate the nearby lookout and clear reserve facilities.

pois.name: Kaituna River Rafting
pois.short: A guided whitewater rafting run through Okere Falls, famous for its steep waterfall drops.
pois.commentary: Kaituna rafting is Rotorua’s water-powered adrenaline story. The river moves fast through green gorge country, and the waterfall drops turn a short trip into something guests tend to remember with wide eyes and wet shoes.

pois.name: Lake Rotomahana Boat Cruise
pois.short: A geothermal lake cruise below Tarawera, exploring the landscape changed by the 1886 eruption.
pois.commentary: Lake Rotomahana is a calm surface over a violent memory. A boat cruise makes the 1886 Tarawera eruption easier to read in the shoreline, steam and coloured cliffs, while the vanished Pink and White Terraces remain the story’s ghostly centre.

pois.name: Lake Tarawera Landing
pois.short: A scenic lakefront stop under Mount Tarawera, linking boat trips, picnics and volcanic history.
pois.commentary: Lake Tarawera Landing gathers several Rotorua threads in one view: deep water, the mountain, boat traffic and the memory of eruption. It works as a launch point, a picnic stop or simply a place to understand why Tarawera still dominates the region’s imagination.

pois.name: Lake Okareka Boardwalk
pois.short: An easy wetland boardwalk on Lake Okareka, with lake views and birdlife close to Rotorua.
pois.commentary: Lake Okareka Boardwalk is Rotorua in a quieter key. Instead of geysers or fast trails, it offers reeds, waterbirds and open lake light, letting visitors take a low-effort walk that still feels connected to the district’s deep lake landscape.

pois.name: Lake Okataina Western Walkway
pois.short: A longer forest walkway above Lake Okataina, linking beaches, bush and volcanic-lake views.
pois.commentary: The Lake Okataina Western Walkway asks for more time than a quick boardwalk, and gives back a more layered Rotorua. The route moves through native forest above the water, with bays and viewpoints that make the lake feel less like a backdrop and more like the centre of the story.

pois.name: Centennial Park
pois.short: A mapped green-space stop that adds local texture to Rotorua.
pois.commentary: Centennial Park fills in another real point on the Rotorua visitor map. It may not be the headline attraction, but these local stops are what make a region feel travelled rather than skimmed: parks, beaches, reserves and viewpoints that give a journey texture between the famous names. OpenStreetMap describes it as Memorial Park with Trees with Headstones & Plaque Benches commemorate Rotorua's loved ones instead of Body Burials. This functions as a Garden Cemetery that has Nature Reserves, Open Farm Paddocks with Sheep roaming around, plus Horse, MB & Event catering.
